content.text: A discriminated union (also called a tagged union) is a set of types that all carry a shared literal property — the discriminant — with a unique value in each member. TypeScript narrows the union to the exact member when you compare the discriminant with a literal. This gives you exhaustive, cast-free handling of every variant.

attrs.alt: Discriminated union anatomy: three variants with a shared status discriminant, and a switch narrowing each to the matching member type

content.text: Three requirements must hold for TypeScript to narrow a union via a discriminant check.
— every member must have a property with the same name (
— the property must have a distinct literal type (string literal, numeric literal, or boolean literal) in each member.
— TypeScript narrows when you compare the discriminant with
By adding an assertNever call in the default branch of a switch over a discriminated union, TypeScript will report a compile-time error if a new union member is added but not handled.

Shared Non-Literal Property Does Not Discriminate
Forgetting the Default in a switch
Nested switch or chained if checks
When a single discriminant is not enough to express all variants, you can nest discriminated unions or use multiple discriminant properties together.
Discriminated Union as a State Machine

A discriminated union is the natural TypeScript encoding of a finite state machine. Each variant represents a state; the discriminant is the state name. Transitions produce a new state value of a different variant.
TypeScript's Extract and Exclude utility types work on discriminated unions member-by-member, enabling precise extraction of individual variants.
require a shared literal-typed property (the discriminant) in every member of the union.
TypeScript narrows to the exact matching member when you compare the discriminant with
in the default branch turns a runtime mistake (unhandled variant) into a compile-time error — add it whenever exhaustive handling is critical.
